Press enter or click to view image in full size

8 min read
Just now

--

A technology architect with no web-development background used AI to build a retirement tax modeling engine. The surprising lesson wasn’t what AI could code — it was how much time validation required.

Two years ago, I wouldn’t have attempted this project. Not because I lacked the idea — but because I lacked the combination of software development experience, retirement tax expertise, and free time required to explore it. And if I’m honest, I assumed that combination was simply the entry fee.
It isn’t anymore.
The first functional version of a retirement tax modeling application came together in roughly twelve hours spread across two evenings after work. That’s the gap I’ve been trying to understand.
While many people were using AI to write better emails, I was building AI agents on AWS. Then I built a website using AI. Then I convinced myself it would be a good idea to build a retirement withdrawal planner with a tax modeling engine from scratch.
My preferred way of learning has always been to find a problem slightly beyond my reach and see what happens. I’ve worked alongside engineering teams for years, but I had never built a web application from scratch before this project. That distinction matters — because if someone who wouldn’t normally attempt this can build it, that tells us something different about where the barrier actually sits now.
This project turned out to be the most educational one yet.

The Problem I Couldn’t Find a Tool For
The idea started with a retirement planning question.
I’m 45, which means retirement is far enough away to plan carefully and close enough to take seriously. I wanted to understand how different withdrawal strategies would affect taxes over decades. The more I looked at existing tools, the more I noticed a pattern.
Most retirement calculators are really investment calculators. They focus on balances, growth rates, and withdrawal percentages. Very few spend meaningful effort modeling taxes.
The scenarios I cared about involved questions like: What happens if someone retires at 55 and uses the Rule of 55 for penalty-free 401(k) withdrawals? How should Roth conversion opportunities be evaluated before Social Security begins? How do Medicare IRMAA surcharges affect conversion decisions? What happens when Required Minimum Distributions begin — currently at age 73? How do inherited retirement accounts affect the family’s total tax burden?
Each of these topics is complicated on its own. The complexity emerges when they interact.
A Roth conversion might reduce future RMDs. That same conversion might increase Medicare premiums two years later. It might also change how much of your Social Security benefit gets taxed. The correct answer often depends on how all of these variables interact across decades.
I found plenty of calculators. I couldn’t find a tool that modeled the complete picture.
Eventually I stopped looking. If AI could genuinely act as a development partner, this seemed like a good test.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Withdrawal Strategies for RetirementWhat the Application Actually Does
Most retirement tools ask one question: how much money will I have? RetireSmart asks a different one: which withdrawal strategy leaves me with the most after-tax wealth — and what does that mean for the family I leave behind?
To answer that, it compares multiple withdrawal strategies — from Roth conversion-heavy approaches to tax-efficient drawdown plans — each modeled against a tax modeling engine built from scratch in TypeScript that handles Social Security taxation, Medicare IRMAA surcharges, RMDs, and Roth conversion optimization across multiple account types simultaneously.
Consider this: a retiree who optimizes purely for their own lifetime taxes might unknowingly leave their children a $200,000 tax bill disguised as an inheritance. The strategy that looks best on paper for the retiree is often not the strategy that minimizes taxes across the family. Most tools never surface that tradeoff. This one does.
In reality, large pre-tax retirement accounts are often future tax liabilities disguised as assets. The taxes don’t disappear — they transfer to whoever inherits them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Withdrawal Source and SchedulePress enter or click to view image in full size

Strategy Comparison
The Most Valuable AI Suggestion Wasn’t About Coding
One of the best ideas in the entire project wasn’t a tax calculation. It was an architectural recommendation.
Early in the design process, AI suggested keeping all sensitive financial information entirely on the client side. The application stores retirement profiles locally in the browser, with client-side state management handling the session. No retirement balances are stored in a database. No Social Security estimates are stored on the server.
I intentionally minimized what the server knows. Authentication, session management, and AI conversations happen there — nothing else. The core retirement calculations — tax engine, RMD logic, and Roth conversion optimization — all run entirely in the browser.
I liked the simplicity of the model. If the server were compromised tomorrow, there would be no stored retirement balances available to steal — because the server never receives them. For a financial planning application handling sensitive personal data, that felt like the right architectural tradeoff.

How I Used AI
The development workflow evolved into a two-layer system.
The first layer was Claude.ai — used as a reasoning partner and, at times, a fellow architect. It didn’t just help work through IRS rules and validate calculations — it suggested technical approaches I hadn’t considered and pushed back on design decisions. Every major decision started there. But the validation was mine. I verified calculations against hand-worked math, pushed back when numbers looked wrong, and treated every output as a starting point rather than a conclusion.
The second layer was Claude Code inside VS Code. Once a decision was made, I used Claude.ai to refine it into a precise implementation prompt — and Claude Code handled the implementation from there: writing TypeScript functions, building React components, fixing compilation issues, and helping with deployment. Each prompt was pre-vetted by the reasoning layer — not “build this” but “build this specific thing we’ve already confirmed is mathematically correct.”
What surprised me wasn’t that the workflow worked. It was how naturally the responsibilities separated. One layer reasoned. One layer implemented.
The human role was orchestration and validation. Sitting between two AI systems, framing problems precisely, deciding which layer to engage for which task, and most critically: verifying that outputs were correct before trusting them. The workflow wasn’t AI writing and me approving. It was two systems with different strengths, collaborating through a human layer responsible for deciding what was worth building and whether the output could be trusted.
I spent far more time deciding what should be built than worrying about how it would be built.

The Bug That Changed My View of AI
The most educational moment in the project came from a bug.
The application included a strategy called RMD Minimization. Its entire purpose was to aggressively convert pre-tax retirement assets into Roth accounts in order to reduce future Required Minimum Distributions.
During a deeper review of the withdrawal logic, Claude identified a subtle issue. A single guard clause contained a hardcoded condition that stopped Roth conversions once the user reached age 73. On the surface, everything looked fine — balances changed, withdrawals occurred, projections appeared reasonable.
But the underlying logic was doing the exact opposite of what the strategy intended.
The strategy named RMD Minimization was quietly producing larger future RMDs, because conversions had stopped precisely when they needed to continue. The irony was hard to miss.
What made this bug particularly instructive was how difficult it would have been to catch casually. Nothing crashed. No obvious error appeared. The numbers looked plausible. They were simply wrong. Without comparing all five withdrawal strategies side-by-side and tracing the logic carefully, it might have gone unnoticed indefinitely.
What fascinated me was that AI helped introduce the bug and later helped find it. That experience changed how I think about AI-assisted development. The value isn’t blind automation. The value is tightening the loop between building something and confirming it actually works.

Validation Took Longer Than Development
This was the biggest surprise of the entire project.
Not the coding. Not the AI. Validation.
The application itself came together remarkably quickly. Proving that the calculations were correct took substantially longer — and that wasn’t what I expected going in.
Every IRS rule required verification. Every edge case required testing. Every year-by-year projection required scrutiny. The more sophisticated the system became, the more important verification became.
The popular narrative around AI focuses on speed. And speed is real. But speed is only useful when paired with confidence. Without validation, faster output simply means arriving at incorrect conclusions more quickly.
AI dramatically accelerated implementation on this project. It did not eliminate the need for skepticism. If anything, it increased it.

What AI Actually Changed
What AI changed was the cost of exploration.
Before AI, this project probably wouldn’t have happened. I wasn’t going to hire a software team, engage a retirement tax specialist, and spend months reading IRS publications to investigate a personal planning question. The cost and effort would have exceeded my curiosity.
AI lowered that barrier enough to make exploration worthwhile. It allowed me to investigate a complex problem that previously would have required access to multiple specialists.
That’s a meaningful shift. Not because expertise has become unnecessary — but because expertise has become more accessible. The differentiator is no longer access to capability. It’s the quality of judgment you bring to it: the ability to frame a problem precisely and recognize when an answer is subtly wrong.
That leverage comes from going deep on a single hard problem — using AI as a genuine thinking partner across weeks, not a shortcut across minutes.
The most underrated skill in any of this isn’t prompting. It’s skepticism.

Final Thoughts
The most memorable moment wasn’t deploying the application. It wasn’t seeing the first successful projection. It wasn’t watching AI generate hundreds of lines of working code.
It was staring at a table of numbers and feeling that something wasn’t quite right.
That instinct led to a bug. The bug led to an investigation. The investigation led to a better understanding of both the retirement model and the AI systems helping build it.
Looking back, the hardest part wasn’t the building at all. It was everything before hands touched keyboard — the ideation, the problem definition, the hours spent asking whether this was even the right thing to build. AI can execute remarkably fast. It can’t tell you what’s worth building in the first place. That part is still entirely human.
That loop — human curiosity, machine assistance, validation, correction — felt like the real story. Not automation. Not replacement. Collaboration. Messy, imperfect, occasionally frustrating collaboration. The kind that produces learning.
The people who benefit most from AI won’t be the ones who outsource their thinking. They’ll be the ones who use it to explore problems they previously couldn’t justify tackling alone.
After spending two evenings building a retirement tax engine as someone who had never built a web application before, that’s the conclusion I keep coming back to:
